title: Reflection — Self-Critique & Iterate
summary: Have the model review its own output, name what's wrong, and try again — turning a one-shot guess into an iterative draft-critique-revise loop that catches its own mistakes.
analogy: A writer editing their first draft. The initial draft is rarely the best; rereading it ('this paragraph is weak, the ending rushes') and rewriting is where quality actually comes from. Reflection gives the model that editor.
problem: A model's first answer is a single forward pass with no chance to check itself. It commits to mistakes — buggy code, flawed reasoning, missed requirements — that it could often catch if it simply looked again.
forces: First-pass generation and careful evaluation are different skills the model can do separately.
Each reflection round adds latency and cost.
Without a real signal, self-critique can be hollow or convince itself it's fine.
Loops can oscillate or never converge without a stop condition.
solution: After producing output, prompt the model to critique it against the goal (or run it against a test/tool for feedback), then revise. Repeat until the critique is satisfied or a budget is hit. Feedback can be self-generated (Self-Refine) or grounded in external signals like test results or error messages (Reflexion).
tradeoffs: More latency and tokens per task — each loop is extra calls.
Ungrounded self-critique can be unreliable; the model may rationalize its own errors.
Needs a clear stop condition or it loops without converging.
Diminishing returns: most gains come in the first one or two iterations.
body:
## First principles
Generating and judging are **different cognitive tasks**. When a model produces an answer in one pass, it never gets to apply its judging ability to its own work — so avoidable errors slip through. Humans don't write final drafts in one go; we draft, reread, and revise. The improvement lives in the **revision loop**, not the first attempt.

Reflection gives the model that loop: produce → **critique** → **revise** → repeat. The critical design choice is **where the feedback comes from**. Pure self-critique (Self-Refine, Madaan et al. 2023) asks the model to find its own flaws. Grounded reflection (Reflexion, Shinn et al. 2023) feeds in **real signals** — test failures, tool errors, environment feedback — which are far more reliable than the model grading itself.

## Plain definition
Reflection = a loop where the model evaluates its own output against the goal (and ideally external feedback), then revises, until it's satisfied or hits a budget.

## Mental model
Draft → critique (self or grounded) → good enough? → if no, revise and loop; if yes, stop. The diagram makes the loop and its exit explicit.

## Self-critique vs. grounded feedback
- **Self-Refine**: model critiques itself. Cheap, no tools; but a model that made an error can also miss it.
- **Reflexion**: critique is grounded in **external** signals (unit tests pass/fail, runtime errors). Much stronger because the feedback is real, not the model's opinion.

## Real examples
- **Coding agents (Cursor, Copilot, Claude Code)**: write code, run tests, read the failure, fix — grounded reflection in action.
- **Reflexion paper**: agents that verbally reflect on failed attempts and retry improved markedly on reasoning and coding benchmarks.

## When to use it
- Output **quality** matters more than speed, and a **check** exists (tests, rubric, validator).
- The task has a clear notion of 'better' the model can evaluate.

## Watch out for
- **Ungrounded critique** can be hollow — prefer real feedback signals when available.
- **No stop condition** → endless loops; cap iterations.
- Reflection is closely related to **evaluator-optimizer**; the difference is mainly whether the critic is the same model or a separate one.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  G([Goal]) --> D[Draft output]
  D --> CR[Critique:\nself or external feedback\n(tests, errors, rubric)]
  CR --> Q{Good enough\nor budget hit?}
  Q -- no --> RV[Revise using critique]
  RV --> CR
  Q -- yes --> F([Final output])

examples.what: Generate code, execute the test suite, read failing tests and stack traces, then revise — a grounded reflection loop using real signals.
Agents verbally reflect on why an attempt failed and store that reflection to guide the next try, boosting reasoning and coding success.

drills.q: Why can a draft-critique-revise loop beat a single-pass answer?
drills.a: Judging is a separate skill from generating; letting the model evaluate and revise its own work catches mistakes the first pass committed to.

drills.q: What is the key difference between Self-Refine and Reflexion?
drills.a: Self-Refine uses the model's own self-critique; Reflexion grounds the critique in external signals like test failures or runtime errors, which is more reliable.

drills.q: What two failure modes must a reflection loop guard against?
drills.a: Hollow ungrounded self-critique (the model rationalizing its error) and non-convergence/infinite looping (needs a stop condition or iteration cap).

teachItBack: Reflection turns a one-shot answer into a draft-critique-revise loop, because judging work is a different skill from producing it and the revision is where quality comes from. The critique can be the model grading itself (Self-Refine) or, far more reliably, grounded in real signals like failing tests and error messages (Reflexion). You cap the loop with a budget so it converges, and you prefer real feedback over self-opinion so the critique isn't hollow.

glossary.definition: A loop where a model critiques its own output and revises it until satisfied or out of budget.
A reflection approach where the same model generates feedback on its output and iterates without external tools.
A reflection approach that grounds critique in external feedback (e.g., test results) and stores reflections to guide retries.

quiz.q: What makes Reflexion-style reflection more reliable than pure self-critique?
choices: Its critique is grounded in external feedback like test failures, not the model's own opinion
quiz.explanation: Grounding critique in real signals (tests, errors) is more trustworthy than a model grading its own work.

quiz.q: What must every reflection loop include to be safe in production?
choices: A stop condition / iteration budget so it converges
quiz.explanation: Without a stop condition the critique-revise loop can run forever; cap iterations or stop when the critique is satisfied.
